speaker: interviewer
text: --with Clara Heller Isaacman. Clara Heller Isaacman, shown here with her dog, is a well-known educated-- educator, I'm sorry-- and author and a much-sought-after public speaker. She is a Holocaust survivor and has only, in recent years, begun to speak out about her experiences during the Holocaust during World War II. She has also written a book, about it and a compilation of stories from other survivors and some of the other people who were involved in the Holocaust. Mrs. Isaacman's aim is to have her story told and to teach lessons of the past to the generations of the future.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to open my remarks with telling you students that I'm not here just to tell a story. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am here to make you aware what life can be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and what you can make of your life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If you, our future generation, will not take care [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and build our United States of America [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as a free country like it should be, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we will have-- and we could have-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the United States, [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] things happening that happened in Europe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what happened in Europe was started by one man. His name was Hitler. And he started with a small group of nine people. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He said, later on, I started as a mouse, and I became an elephant. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Why did he become an elephant? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because he took away [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the individual thinking of each one of his people. They became his people. He became their God. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They didn't-- he didn't let them do, anymore, what wanted to do. They had to do what wanted to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, it sounded very good. He gave them food, clothing, uniforms, power. And all of this for young boys and girls-- great. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the most important thing that he took away from them, they didn't realize until it was too late. And that's a word that all of you are using very freely. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The word is freedom. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We use it, and we really don't know what it means until we lose it. Today, I'd like to give you a few examples, through pictures, what it means to be free and how great it is for all of you and for me to be an American and to be an American Jew. More than that-- to be a survivor who can tell the story to the American students, Jews and non-Jews. And I hope that because of the few examples that I will give you, you will learn and be able to teach the rest of the generations to come. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Look at picture 1. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's a little girl who is reading a book. It's one of the stories in my book. This little girl has lost her father, her mother, her brother. She has nothing else but the bed, no curtains, no beauty there, but a friend-- the book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did she lose her father and mother and brother? Because she was born Jewish. Because the world-- Hitler with his friends-- decided that one person is better than the other. And Hitler wanted a pure race.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Look at picture 2. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] If you look real good, there's a clock there-- time, people sitting around and trying very hard to gain time because they are circled around with enemies. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They don't have-- you don't see there are too many flowers, do you? You see, here and there, a tree. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they hoped that it will grow, have fruit. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But Hitler didn't give them the chance. The people are discussing time and how much time they have to still be free and sit together-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe an hour, maybe a day.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went into hiding, I didn't know how much time I will be in hiding. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I was hidden for two and a half years. What does that to mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It means, to me, I didn't see the daylight. I didn't feel fresh air. I didn't know the difference of the four seasons. I would've loved to feel grass [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or snow on my body or a rain drop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Time-- how much time do you have to make the world better? Don't say 'tomorrow.' Start today.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] If you go to picture 3, [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] look real good and see how close those two people are. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am going to call them mother and child. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They have nothing around them. There's not a roof on top [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and nothing on the bottom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are they? Imagine yourself in a very narrow place, in a hiding place, where you can't breathe [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and you can't move. You are waiting for somebody to help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe somebody will come. And most of the people will say, no. Why? Why did they have to hide there? Because they were born Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's look at the other picture-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] not easy to see it. But one thing that you really can see very strongly is a swastika. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, if you take that swastika and turn it around, it's a twisted cross. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: People wouldn't want to see that. They want to feel that they never had anything to do with the swastika. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But let's all remember [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that any twisted cross who had to do with the swastika helped Hitler. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go to the next picture. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This young little girl [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was taken away by her parents-- from her parents, sent to Norway to a farm, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] didn't know the language, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and had to work day and night to make her worth to be able to eat a piece of bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a lucky little girl. She at least survived the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But how many little girls and boys [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] did not have food? And I mean not having food-- not for an hour or a day, but for a few days-- and not knowing that you can go into a store or home and eating.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are lucky. You leave school. Or you go someplace and you want to eat. Then you walk home, you open the refrigerator, and it's full of food.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you say, there is nothing to eat. You know why you're saying that? Because there's nothing there you like. But there is plenty to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: This little girl had something to eat. My friends who died and perished during the war only because they were born Jews had nothing to eat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go to the next picture. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] What do you see there? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mother isn't there anymore. Father, maybe Uncle, who knows? Another man with three children.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They're not too happy. But they're not too sad because they still feel that they have somebody around. But Daddy knows Mommy is no more alive. The Germans took her, killed her, slaughtered her. Why? Because she was born Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you look at all those pictures and the children, they look just like you. They're not much different. They may be dressed a little bit different. But if you think hard, they were just like you. They had a home. They had parents. They had a bicycle. They were just like you. They went to school, just like you. They wanted to play, just like you. But Hitler killed them all and took them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's look at the next picture. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] This picture [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is telling you a story [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that is in my other book. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture, I couldn't have made it. It's too much-- too beautiful. I'm not an artist.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it tells you what happened to me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It tells you how Hitler chased me out of my house. I went to look for refuge. And I found no place. Even the horse and the wagon, I had to give away-- my parents had to give away because we couldn't feed the horse. I will tell you more about this when I see you. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] This picture, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] if you look real good, has a peace bird. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's telling you-- giving you a message. But the rest of it also has a very great message. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One message is that Hitler always started on our Sabbath.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Friday night. The table was set. Everything was peaceful in the house. And Hitler destroyed the Friday night. How did he do it? I am going to tell you one little story [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the chapter of my book.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One Friday night, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we were sitting at our table having our Sabbath meal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we heard trucks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They stopped with such a strength that nobody could dream or realize what's coming. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was blackouts outside. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] All at once, we heard shouting, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] crying. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My Daddy got up from the table, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] peeked out, and said, oh my god! This is it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he let us look. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What did we see? [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] We saw German soldiers [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] having flames in their hands, torches, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] going from door to door, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and taking everybody out of the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My friends and children were crying, Mommy, where are you? I want to go with you in the truck. Daddy, where are you? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Please, don't pull her by the hair. She's paralyzed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they filled the trucks with young and old and pregnant women.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When they got to our house, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I opened the door for them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were lucky. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They asked my parents for their passport. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my parents gave it to them. They looked at each other and said, tonight, we are not taking Romanian Jews. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was hidden for two-and-a-half years. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I did not have a home like you have. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I did not have a bed like you have. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would like you to remember that it doesn't matter how many rooms you have or how many dresses or shoes you have. The most important thing to have is freedom. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We talk about a lot of words. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You have heard the word 'prejudice.' You have heard the word 'intolerance.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You have heard the word 'hatred.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What is really hating? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Do I hate you? I can't hate you or love you. I don't know you. But Hitler hated all the Jews. He hated everybody who didn't think like he did. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Should we hate somebody who is a different religion? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No. All of you should be proud, no matter what group you belong to. Feel good about it. Then, you will know that everybody can live together, even if we are not all friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: There are other words-- 'rescue.' [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When should we run and help somebody? And when shouldn't we? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I feel that if anybody asks for help, we should help because you never know when you will need the help.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: There are words that we use so freely, and we don't understand-- 'racism,' 'bigotry.' Oh, he's a racist. He's a bigot. Should we hate all people that we don't know? Should hate them because they belong to a certain group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I am a survivor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The most important message to you is read, study, understand. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And let's go to the next picture. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's not so easy to see there. But what it is, really-- a celebration of a lot of Jewish holidays. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't celebrate them for a long time. You celebrate yours. And I hope that the world will let me celebrate mine. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is the Belgium flag framed on a citation that I got from the Belgium King. And it says, 'In honor for Mademoiselle Heller, who fought against the Germans and participated in the resistance of liberating Belgium, 1940-1945,' signed by the Belgian King. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I would like to finish with a picture [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of the Holocaust that was made by an artist, very known one. Look at it. Read into it. Don't walk away from it, even if it scares you a little. This is what Hitler wanted to do to the world and to most of us who had done nothing wrong. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] This was given to me by my daddy. He bought it when I was born. He put it away for my trousseau. That's the only thing I have left that is materialistic, that I can look and say, my daddy bought this for me.
